zh:
凯尔特神话主要指爱尔兰与不列颠盖尔语区流传下来的神话与传说。它的源头在铁器时代的口传信仰，但凯尔特的德鲁伊祭司阶层不立文字，今天读到的版本几乎全部出自公元7至12世纪爱尔兰修道院僧侣的抄录——《褐牛之书》《伦斯特书》《入侵之书》是最重要的几部抄本。基督徒抄经人把旧日的神写成远古的移民与英雄，于是达努神族在《入侵之书》里成了爱尔兰的第五批征服者：他们战胜深海的弗摩尔族，又败给凡人米尔的子民，退入史前坟丘之下，化作民间信仰里的仙灵一族（aos sí）。

学界习惯把爱尔兰材料分为四个故事群：讲述诸神来历与莫伊图拉大战的神话故事群；以库丘林和史诗《夺牛记》为中心、血气翻涌的阿尔斯特故事群；围绕芬恩·麦克库尔及其游猎战团、笔调转向山林与哀歌的芬恩故事群；以及诸王传说。威尔士的《马比诺吉昂》则保存了不列颠一支的回声。这套神话没有奥林波斯式的整饬秩序：神明会被打败、受伤、被凡人讨价还价，边界感极薄的彼岸世界就藏在坟丘之下、湖水之中、西海尽头的雾里，萨温节前后两界的门户敞开。

中世纪文本之外，凯尔特想象在民间层面一直活着：报丧的班西、水中的凯尔派、褪皮的塞尔基、藏金的矮妖，都是近两百年民俗采录里仍在呼吸的形象。19世纪末的凯尔特文艺复兴把这批材料推入现代文学，叶芝、格雷戈里夫人的整理让它成为爱尔兰民族想象的底色，也为现代奇幻文学输送了大量母题与形象。
en:
Celtic mythology, as it survives, is above all the mythology of the Gaelic world of Ireland and Britain. Its roots lie in Iron Age oral religion, but the druids committed nothing to writing, and nearly everything we read today was set down between the seventh and twelfth centuries by Christian monks in Irish monasteries, in manuscripts such as the Book of the Dun Cow, the Book of Leinster and the Book of Invasions. The scribes recast the old gods as ancient settlers and heroes: in the Book of Invasions the Tuatha De Danann arrive as Ireland's fifth wave of conquerors, defeat the Fomorians from the sea, lose the island to the mortal sons of Mil, and withdraw beneath the prehistoric burial mounds, where folk belief kept them alive as the aos si, the fairy folk.

Scholars sort the Irish material into four cycles: the Mythological Cycle of the gods and the battles of Mag Tuired; the Ulster Cycle, hot-blooded and heroic, centred on Cu Chulainn and the epic Tain Bo Cuailnge; the Fenian Cycle of Fionn mac Cumhaill and his roving war-band, tuned to woodland, hunting and elegy; and the Cycles of the Kings. The Welsh Mabinogi preserves a British branch of the same inheritance. There is no Olympian tidiness here: gods can be wounded, beaten and bargained with, and the Otherworld lies not above the clouds but under the next mound, beneath the lake, behind the mist at the edge of the western sea, its doors standing open at Samhain.

Beyond the medieval texts the Celtic imagination stayed alive at ground level, in the keening banshee, the kelpie in the ford, the selkie shedding her skin, the leprechaun and his crock of gold, figures still breathing in two centuries of folklore collection. The Celtic Revival of the late nineteenth century, through Yeats and Lady Gregory, carried this material into modern literature, where it colours Ireland's national imagination and feeds much of modern fantasy.
